ina1 filed in 87100-3 




Mr. Ted Prim 
Fair Political Practice Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 
98514 
Dear Mr. Prim: 
March 24, 1981 
This letter is written to ask for an opinion from your office as to 
whether I mayor may not continue to serve on the California Horse Racing 
Commission. 
The main question revolves around a potential conflict of 
interest, the facts of which are as follows: 
I am in the Real Estate investment business and have been so for 15 
years. 
I specialize in buying investment properties for myself and a se-
lect list of clients. 
During the month of January I was contacted by Mr. 
Lloyd Arnold who wanted me to look for a Real Estate investment opportun-
ity for a company in which he is a stockholder, namely the Illini Beef 
Company of Illinois. 
The Illini Beef Company is a public held company 
located in Geneseo, Illinois. 
Their stock is traded over the counter and 
they have been in business for a number of years. 
Mr. Arnold informed me 
that he was a stockholder, but did not control the company nor was he on 
the Board of Directors. 
During the month of February an investment CaIne to light that I have 
wanted to purchase for quite some time, namely 670 acres located in Yolo 
County. 
I contacted Mr. Gus Lohman, the Chairman of the Board of Illini 
Beef and appraised him of the investment and he agreed to come to Sacramento 
to look at it to see if his company would have some interest. 
Mr. Lohman 
flew to Sacramento and, along with Mr. Arnold and myself, reviewed the in-
vestment. 
After Mr. Lohman's visit, I felt that his company was not interested, 
so I 
to raise the money needed here locally, with my own investors. 
Some time during that week, Mr. Lohman called me and said his Board would 
be interested if I would put up half of the money needed to purchase the 
I told him that that would not be a problem and he then said his 
Board would meet and he would let me know of their interest. 
within that 
Mr. Lohman 
me and made an 
for his Board to 
to Sacramento to review the 
did so the 
and after a day-long meeting, 
to have a closed Board 
7700 COLLEGE TOWN DRIVE, SUITE 
CALIFORNIA 95826 
383-8090 


Mr. Ted Prim 
Page 2 
March 24, 1981 
Meeting and voted to pick up half of the investment. 
We have formed a Joint 
Venture between Illini Beef and South Port Investors, a California Limited 
Partnership, of which I am the General Partner, and purchased the property. 
My question is this: 
because of Lloyd Arnold's involvement as a stock-
holder in Illini Beef Company and also being licensed by the State of California 
to run Harness Meets at various State tracks which are regulated by the 
California Horse Racing Commission, of which I am a member, do I face a potential 
conflict of interest? 
I have talked to my own attorney and to the Attorney General's office and 
both feel that this is not in any way a conflict of interest. 
It is also my 
understanding that Mr. Fuller of the Attorney General's office has spoken with 
you about the same. 
I would like your opinion as quickly as possible so I 
may know what I must do. 
I should also point out that I have resigned from the Commission, but the 
Governor refuses to accept my resignation. 
I have told him nothing of this 
matter and will not do so until I hear from you. 
Many thanks for your consideration. 
Sincerely,

Tentative land pact aids port 
Agreement could open 1 ,000 acres to commercial and industrial uses 
By Max Miller 
Bee Staff Writer 
An "agreement in principle" between the Port 
of Sacramento and a partnership of investors Is 
being hailed as a move that could open 1,000 
acres of land to industrial, commercial and other 
uses. 
The move is expected to benefit both the port 
and the new city of West Sacramento. 
The Sacramento-Yolo Port CommiSSion has ap-
proved preliminary negotiations with Southport 
Investors, a limited partnership headed by bank-
er-developer Frank C Ramos and investor Lloyd 
Arnold, for several seasons the promoter of har-
ness racing at Cal Expo. 
Southport Investors owns 700 acres of land 
stretching for 4 ~ miles along the east bank of the 
Sacramento Deep Water Channel immediately 
south of the harbor in West Sacramento. 
To improve access to the Southport property, 
the Port Commission has preliminarily agreed to 
Protest

sation. I hate to see a response to 
people who commit such acts or con-
done such acts." 
Earlier in the morning, it ap-
peared that ARDAC's prediction of a 
"major demonstration" at the UCD 
Primate Center was a bust 
By 9 a.m., about 20 protesters had 
assembled at the center, about three 
miles west of the main campus, 
where tbey marched and chanted 
_._ .... _--
grant easements across port-owned land in ex-
change for Southport's commitment to build le-
vees and provide areas to deposit dredge material 
from the first phase of a project to deepen the 40-
mile-long ship channel to 35 feet 
Port Director Dennis Clark said the arrange-
ment would save the port "considerable money" 
in the building of levees and the acquisition of an 
estimated 200 to 250 acres of land to store materi-
al dredged from the ship channel. 
The port owns 400 acres of land west of the Ar-
lington Heights subdivision and the Ship channel 
on the south bank of the harbor. 
Clark said the agreement with SOl.lthport Is the 
first in a series of moves to implement West Sac-
ramento's general plan calling for a bridge 
linking commerCial and industrial faCilities on 
the north side of the port and ship channel with 
the Southport area. 
Plans under study call for a bridge across the 
ship channel at the foot of Industrial Boulevard 
about 1 ~ miles east of the present bridge, which 
crosses the barge canal on Jefferson Boulevard. 
Anothe. proposal calls for use of port property 
to locate a 2 million-gallon water tank for the city 
of West Sacramento. 
West Sacramento City Manager Gene Roh said 
the tank will be one of three to be constructed to 
improve the city's water treatment system. 
"The port really has worked with us very well,'7 
Roh said. "We have been trying to keep these 
tanks out of residential areas. 
"Without question we are very supportive of the 
idea of the bridge going in and we are exploring 
the possibility that it could be a part of the city's 
redevelopment project." 
In addition to the preliminary arrangement 
with Southport Investors, the Port CommiSSion ap-
proved the lease of 5,000 square feet of space in 
the port's World Trade Center building to hpuse 
West Sacramento city offices. 
Officials of Southport Investors were not avail-
able Friday for comment on the negotiations with 
the Port Commission.

sections.question: My question is this: because of Lloyd Arnold's involvement as a stockholder in Illini Beef Company and also being licensed by the State of California to run Harness Meets at various State tracks which are regulated by the California Horse Racing Commission, of which I am a member, do I face a potential conflict of interest?
sections.question_synthetic: The writer asks whether serving on the California Horse Racing Commission while engaged in a real estate joint venture with Illini Beef Company (a company in which Lloyd Arnold is a stockholder and who is also licensed to run harness meets at state tracks) creates a conflict of interest.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not a response. The document contains the question/inquiry from the requester. No FPPC response or conclusion is included in this document. The document also contains unrelated newspaper clippings appended at the end which do not pertain to the FPPC inquiry. Confidence reduced slightly due to document organization and appended unrelated materials.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request to the FPPC from a California Horse Racing Commission member asking whether his participation in a real estate investment joint venture with Illini Beef Company, whose stockholder Lloyd Arnold operates harness racing meets at state-regulated tracks, creates a conflict of interest.
